content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

July 13, 1990

Honorable Kenneth L. Maddy
Senate Republican Leader
State Capitol, Room 305
Sacramento, CA 95814

RE: Your Request for Informal Advice
Our File No. I-90-454

Dear Senator Maddy:

This is in response to your request for advice concerning
whether, under the Political Reform Act (the "Act"),1 a
contributor can place limitations on a candidate's use of his or
her contribution. Since your letter appears to present a
hypothetical question, we treat your request as one for informal
assistance under Regulation 18329(b)(8)(D).2

QUESTION

A candidate receives a contribution check which is
accompanied by the following statement from the contributor:

"This contribution can only be used for the campaign of the
recipient."

Does this statement by the contributor prohibit the candidate
from transferring all or a portion of the contribution to a ballot
measure committee controlled by the same candidate, controlled by
another candidate or not controlled by a candidate?

1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section
83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916)322-5660

Our File No. I-90-454
Page 2


CONCLUSION

The Act permits a candidate who has made a particular
ballot measure an issue in his or her campaign to transfer
his or her campaign funds to a committee that supports or
opposes the ballot measure, whether or not controlled by that
candidate, another candidate or no candidate at all. The
Commission staff views this type of transfer as an
appropriate use of the candidate's contributions for seeking
political office. In addition, the Act contains no
requirement that a candidate expend a contribution in a
manner prescribed by the contributor.

ANALYSIS

The Act currently contains certain restrictions upon how
a candidate may expend campaign contributions. Section
85202(b) states that contributions are "deemed to be held in
trust" for expenses associated with the election of the
candidate to the office he or she seeks or expenses
associated with holding that office. Section 85304 prohibits
a candidate from transferring contributions to other
candidates.³ Furthermore, Sections 85800 through 85807
(commonly know as the "personal use law") generally seek to
limit the personal benefit a candidate can derive from
expending his or her campaign contributions.

Recently, the Commission determined that candidates do
not violate the transfer prohibition of Section 85304 in
using their campaign funds to make contributions to a ballot
measure committee controlled by another candidate (Hitchck
Advice Letter, No. 89-533, dated March 16, 1990, copy
enclosed.) The same letter stated that it was permissible
for a candidate to make similar transfers to ballot measure
committees not controlled by another candidate. Implicit in
this analysis is the view that a candidate's expenditure of
campaign funds for this purpose is an expenditure associated
with the candidate's campaign for office, as required by the
trust provisions in Section 85202(b). In the Milman Advice
Letter, No. I-90-206 (copy enclosed), the Commission staff
further clarified the requirements of the trust provision,
stating that, to satisfy the provision, a candidate's
expenditure of campaign funds must be directly related to the
candidate's campaign for office, or be associated with

_______________________

³ Section 85304 also prevents a candidate from using contributions
received in running for one office to run for another office.
However, in the case of Service Employees International Union, et
al. v. FPPC (U.S. District Court, Eastern District of California,
Case No. CIVS 89-0433 LJK-JFM) the court has issued a preliminary
injunction prohibiting enforcement of that provision.

Our File No. I-90-454
Page 3


holding office. On the basis of these letters, we conclude that
where a candidate transfers contributions made to him or her to a
ballot measure committee, it is a permissible expenditure for the
purpose of seeking office as long as the candidate has made that
ballot measure an issue in his or her campaign. Consistent with
the Hiltachk letter, a transfer of this type is permitted
regardless of whether it is to a ballot measure committee
controlled by the candidate, another candidate or no candidate at
all.

Applying this analysis to your question, it is clear that,
since the type of transfer described above is deemed to be
associated with seeking office, it would be "for the campaign of
the recipient" and thus permissible under the restriction placed
on the use of the contribution by your hypothetical contributor.

A larger question presented under the facts set forth in your
letter is whether a contributor can make a campaign contribution
to a candidate upon condition that the contribution be used for a
specific purpose. The Act sets forth only specified uses that may
be made of campaign contributions. It does not prohibit the use
of contributions in a manner contrary to the expressed desire of a
contributor. Therefore, when a candidate spends a contribution in
such a manner, that conduct alone does not result in a violation
of the Act.

Finally, please note that the Commission only has
jurisdiction over areas of the law that are encompassed by the
Act. While a legal argument could be made that a contribution
given under express conditions is subject to contract law or
the law of negotiable instruments, we have no jurisdiction over this
question and thus offer no opinion. Our research has revealed no
California case law on this point to which we can direct you.

I hope this reply has been of assistance. Should you have
additional questions, please contact me at (916) 322-5901.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel

SH:gb

Attachments
